content:
Coding can be a rewarding skill to learn, but it can be hard to know where to begin and where to find information. It’s normal to feel overwhelmed with the prospect of learning something new, especially something as complex as coding. If you’ve looked at code before and thought, “There is no way I will ever be able to understand this,” you aren’t alone, and it is possible. To help with some of these anxieties, here is an honest look at what to expect and what I would suggest for an absolute beginner.
What is Creative Coding
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A ‘sketch’ I created in ProcessingCreative coding is the use of code as a form of creative expression. Examples include algorithmic art and interactive visuals. If this sounds interesting to you, creative coding might be worth exploring. To help you explore it, here are some popular libraries and tools to get started:
Some popular libraries:

• p5.js — a popular JavaScript library designed to be accessible to non-programmers. The library includes objects that help create sketches that run in the web browser. It also includes math-related functions, sound tools, and 3D support. p5.js sketches can be embedded on any web page. There is a lively community, and this library is updated often, so there is always something new to try. (They also have a dedicated web editor)

• Processing (deprecated) — The predecessor to p5.js, Processing runs on Java. It is technically deprecated and no longer receives updates, but Java’s superior performance makes Processing a powerful tool for creating more complex sketches. It features its own dedicated IDE with a debugger.

• Three.js — Another popular JavaScript library, three.js, allows you to load, animate, and interact with 3D objects right on the web. It has an advanced 3D renderer that can handle complex lighting and textures.

• TouchDesigner — TouchDesigner is a node-based visual programming language that lets you create highly interactive visuals.
If you’ve never coded a day in your life. Stop and try a quick tutorial to get you excited about learning.
Is it hard to learn to code?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Gabriel Heinzer on Unsplash“Programming isn’t about what you know; it’s about what you can figure out.” — Chris Pine
I’m going to give you a hard truth: no matter what anyone says, programming is hard. It’s not something you can pick up in a week. It takes time and dedication before you start to understand what you are doing. That being said, it’s not impossible. Programming is a skill that anybody can learn. And if you treat it as a skill to practice every day, you will find yourself progressing quickly.
It took me about a year to get to where I’m at currently. But how fast you learn depends on many factors, such as how much time you realistically have to dedicate to learning and how intrinsically motivated you feel.
Choosing a Language
Learning a new programming language is not like learning a spoken language, such as starting with Chinese and then moving to French. I would say that programming languages are more like closely related dialects, with some key differences between them. The point is, you don’t need to feel that if you choose one language, you will be pigeonholed for life. Choose a language and stick with it until you’re comfortable. Once you get to that level, you will find that crossing over to another language is a lot easier than you’d think.
Ultimately, it usually comes down to what you want to build. If you are excited about building things on the web, go with JavaScript. If you want to program games, you might want to try C#, or maybe you want to program physical hardware, in which case Python might be your go-to language. The best language for you is the one that gets you most excited about learning. That being said, JavaScript is a good option for beginners. There are many online JavaScript editors you can play around with at any time, so the barrier to entry is lower. JavaScript is also dynamically typed, which is a little more forgiving.
Where to start
There are countless bootcamps and starter guides out there, and with a topic as complex as programming, you might fall into the trap of feeling like you need to dissect and understand everything from the ground up before you can start making. My suggestion is to find a textbook for your chosen programming language and start working through it. A good textbook is filled with projects and examples throughout, so you are building things as you learn. Prioritize making and actually typing out the code examples.
The first two books I started with were Eloquent JavaScript and The Nature of Code. Both are available online for free and have their own unique approach to teaching. The Nature of Code teaches through real-world simulations, which I found very compelling, but I would definitely recommend learning the basics before jumping into a book like this.
Feeling Stuck
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Vitaly Gariev on UnsplashLearning to code is difficult, and there are an endless number of new things to learn. Although it can be gratifying to rattle your brain until you find a solution to a problem, it’s important to be able to recognize when you are truly burned out or have hit a wall. Take a break, go outside, or do something unrelated for a while. I have taken weeks away from programming, and upon returning, I feel my skills have improved. Sometimes your brain needs some time to process what it’s learned.
It’s completely normal to feel like you’ve hit a rut in your progress. You might not feel like you’re learning, but you are. Find ways to measure your progress along the way, and you might be surprised how much you’ve advanced. Most importantly, make sure you still feel motivated. It’s important to learn how to solve difficult problems and finish projects, but if you find yourself completely uninterested, move on. You won’t learn if you don’t feel excited about what you’re making. You might also join a community, forum, or group where you can meet like-minded people and help each other.
How about AI?
I think that to get the most from AI, you need to understand what it can and cannot do. Handing over a project entirely, or trying to get AI to solve a complex problem for you, will almost certainly end in headaches. What seems to work at first will slowly become a nightmare, and you may find yourself relying on a process that gets things wrong. If you can’t solve a problem on your own, it is rarely useful to get AI to fix it for you. If the problem I’m trying to solve is significantly more complex than something akin to spellcheck, I won’t rely on AI for that.
For example, I sometimes copy and paste code snippets to weed out a syntax error, but I won’t ask it to add a method to my code or ‘fix a problem’. I have tried it countless times, and I am here to tell you it won’t end well. This doesn’t mean you need to figure everything out yourself. If you’re really stuck, learn how to read documentation or search forums. And if you’re really brave, you can try asking someone for help.
Passing the Learning Curve
“It turns out that style matters in programming for the same reason that it matters in writing. It makes for better reading.” — Douglas Crockford, JavaScript: The Good Parts.
You will get to a point where you feel you can be let ‘off the leash’ and create without needing any reference. This is a good time to take inventory of your skills and make sure you know everything you need to keep yourself (and others) sane. Having well-structured, well-organized code will make the creative process flow much more easily. You will find yourself creating not just functions but entire systems and worlds! Make sure you can understand your own code. Style guides can be found online, and make sure you understand programming concepts like OOP before you truly set out on your coding journey on your own.
So what now?
The best first step is the one that moves you forward. To help you choose, here are some places you might start:

• Open the first chapter of a textbook

• Follow a coding tutorial

• Just play around in a code editor

• Join a community

• Follow a blog (like mine!)
Learning a new skill is challenging, but extremely rewarding. The most important thing is to show up consistently and, most importantly, have fun!
